title: User guide
text:
This section explains how to use pyansys-tools-report and its features.
Once the pyansys-tools-report package is installed in your own personal environment (if not, please proceed
to ref_getting_started), you can start importing the package and using it.
In order to import the package, please run:
The pyansys-tools-report contains a main class called Report. This class is basically an extension of the
scooby.Report, but customized for showing Ansys libraries and variables in a common format.
The possible arguments that the Report class accepts can be found in the ref_index_api. PLease, have a look
at it to understand the arguments you may use. A simple example of Ansys variables and libraries is shown below.
The typical output of a Report would look as follows:
By default, the Report class would look for a certain set of environment variables. The following
strings are searched for in the available environment variables. If any match is found, they are included
in the report:
AWP_ROOT
ANS
MAPDL
FLUENT
AEDT
DPF
Also, several Python packages are reported by default. The set of reported packages always includes
the following list:
ansys-mapdl-core
ansys-dpf-core
ansys-dpf-post
ansys-dpf-gate
ansys-fluent-core
pyaedt
ansys-platform-instancemanagement
ansys-grantami-bomanalytics
ansys-openapi-common
ansys-mapdl-reader
ansys-fluent-visualization
ansys-fluent-parametric
ansys-sphinx-theme
ansys-seascape
pyansys-tools-report
pyansys-tools-versioning
matplotlib
numpy
pyvista
appdirs
tqdm
pyiges
scipy
grpc
google.protobuf
If you want the Report class to look for some extra environment variables by default, please
raise an issue.
Enjoy its use. If you have any doubts, please raise a question/issue in the
PyAnsys Tools Report Issues site.
import ansys.tools.report as pyansys_report
# After defining my_ansys_libs and my_ansys_vars with the needed format (see API Reference)
# we can start the instantiation of the report
#
# Instantiate a Report object
rep = report.Report(ansys_libs=my_ansys_libs, ansys_vars=my_ansys_vars)

# For printing the report
rep
>>> -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
>>> PyAnsys Software and Environment Report
>>> -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
>>> Date: Wed Nov 30 14:54:58 2022 Romance Standard Time
>>>
>>>                                OS : Windows
>>>                            CPU(s) : 16
>>>                           Machine : AMD64
>>>                      Architecture : 64bit
>>>                       Environment : Python
>>>                        GPU Vendor : Intel
>>>                      GPU Renderer : Intel(R) UHD Graphics
>>>                       GPU Version : 4.5.0 - Build 30.0.100.9955
>>>
>>> Python 3.10.4 (tags/v3.10.4:9d38120, Mar 23 2022, 23:13:41) [MSC v.1929 64 bit (AMD64)]
>>>
>>>                  ansys-mapdl-core : X.Y.Z
>>>                    ansys-dpf-core : X.Y.Z
>>>                    ansys-dpf-post : X.Y.Z
>>>                    ansys-dpf-gate : X.Y.Z
>>>                 ansys-fluent-core : X.Y.Z
>>>                            pyaedt : X.Y.Z
>>> ansys-platform-instancemanagement : X.Y.Z
>>>       ansys-grantami-bomanalytics : X.Y.Z
>>>              ansys-openapi-common : X.Y.Z
>>>                ansys-mapdl-reader : X.Y.Z
>>>        ansys-fluent-visualization : X.Y.Z
>>>           ansys-fluent-parametric : X.Y.Z
>>>                ansys-sphinx-theme : X.Y.Z
>>>                    ansys-seascape : X.Y.Z
>>>              pyansys-tools-report : X.Y.Z
>>>          pyansys-tools-versioning : X.Y.Z
>>>                        matplotlib : X.Y.Z
>>>                             numpy : X.Y.Z
>>>                           pyvista : X.Y.Z
>>>                           appdirs : X.Y.Z
>>>                              tqdm : X.Y.Z
>>>                            pyiges : X.Y.Z
>>>                             scipy : X.Y.Z
>>>                              grpc : X.Y.Z
>>>                   google.protobuf : X.Y.Z
>>>
>>>
>>> -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
>>> Ansys Environment Report
>>> -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
>>>
>>>
>>> Ansys Installation
>>> ******************
>>> Version   Location
>>> ------------------
>>> MyLib1       v1.2
>>> MyLib2       v1.3
>>>
>>>
>>> Ansys Environment Variables
>>> ***************************
>>> MYVAR_1                        VAL_1
>>> MYVAR_2                        VAL_2

title: Getting started
text:
To install pyansys-tools-report, carefully read all instructions on this page.
This package may be installed following two procedures: either the pip package manager installation or
the manual installation. The process to be followed for each of them is shown in the upcoming sections.
The pyansys-tools-report package currently supports Python >=3.10 on Windows, Mac OS, and Linux.
Install the latest release from PyPi with:
Alternatively, install the latest from GitHub via:
For a local “development” version, install with (requires pip >= 22.0):
If you lack an internet connection on your install machine, the recommended way
of installing PyAnsys Tools Report is downloading the wheelhouse archive from the
Releases Page for your
corresponding machine architecture.
Each wheelhouse archive contains all the python wheels necessary to install
PyAnsys Tools Report from scratch on Windows and Linux for Python >=3.10. You can install
this on an isolated system with a fresh python or on a virtual environment.
For example, on Linux with Python 3.10, unzip it and install it with the following:
If you’re on Windows with Python 3.10, unzip to a wheelhouse directory and
install using the same command as before.
Consider installing using a virtual environment.
More information on general PyAnsys development can be found in the
PyAnsys Developer’s Guide.
This repository basically contains a simple Python package which you can import as follows
(once installed):
Once imported, you can then start playing around with it:
Refer to the ref_index_api to see the details of the module.
pip install pyansys-tools-report
pip install git+https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git
git clone https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git
cd pyansys-tools-report
pip install .
unzip pyansys-tools-report-v0.9.dev0-wheelhouse-Linux-3.10.zip wheelhouse
pip install pyansys-tools-report -f wheelhouse --no-index --upgrade --ignore-installed
import ansys.tools.report as pyansys_report
# Instantiate a "default" Report
rep = pyansys_report.Report()

title: PyAnsys Tools Report
text:
Find here below the contents of the repository’s main README file.
PyAnsys
Python
PyPi
codecov
GH-CI
MIT
Black
pre-commit.ci status
Ansys tool for reporting your Python environment’s package versions and hardware resources in a standardized way.
PyAnsys Tools Report
Table of contents
Introduction
Documentation and issues
How does it work?
Installation
Standard installation
Offline installation
Rendering the docs
Running the tests
Requirements
The PyAnsys Tools Report is a PyAnsys package to homogenize among all the different repositories
the output of system and environment reports related to Ansys products. Its main goals are:
Provide an homogeneous output style to system and environment reports.
Allows for customization when reporting Ansys variables and libraries.
You are welcome to help contribute to it in any possible way. Please submit an issue with
any proposal you may have.
See the documentation page for more details.
You are welcome to help contribute to it in any possible way. Please submit
here an issue with
any proposal you may have. This is the best place to post questions and code.
This repository basically contains a simple Python package which you can import as follows
(once installed):
Once imported, you can then start playing around with it:
Refer to the online documentation to see the details of the module.
This package may be installed following two procedures: either the pip package manager installation or
the manual installation. The process to be followed for each of them is shown in the upcoming sections.
The pyansys-tools-report package currently supports Python >=3.10 on Windows, Mac OS, and Linux.
Install the latest release from PyPi with:
Alternatively, install the latest from GitHub via:
For a local “development” version, install with (requires pip >= 22.0):
If you lack an internet connection on your install machine, the recommended way
of installing PyAnsys Tools Report is downloading the wheelhouse archive from the
Releases Page for your
corresponding machine architecture.
Each wheelhouse archive contains all the python wheels necessary to install
PyAnsys Tools Report from scratch on Windows and Linux for Python >=3.10. You can install
this on an isolated system with a fresh python or on a virtual environment.
For example, on Linux with Python 3.10, unzip it and install it with the following:
If you’re on Windows with Python 3.10, unzip to a wheelhouse directory and
install using the same command as before.
Consider installing using a virtual environment.
More information on general PyAnsys development can be found in the
PyAnsys Developer’s Guide.
In case you were interested in rendering the docs locally, you need to clone the repository and
install the docs requirements first:
Once you have the requirements, render the docs by running the following:
This generates the HTML version of the docs, which you may find in the following directory:
You can also clean the build directory by running the following command:
In case you were interested in running the tests locally, you need to clone the repository and
install the test requirements first:
Once you have the requirements, run the tests by running the following:
The pyproject.toml file already contains some default configuration for running the tests. Please,
take a look at it if you may want to run it with your own configuration.
This Python package does not contain specific requirements files. Instead, its requirements may
be found within the pyproject.toml file which defines the package. Thus, when the package is
installed it automatically detects the dependencies needed and installs them.
import ansys.tools.report as pyansys_report

# Instantiate a "default" Report
rep = pyansys_report.Report()

   pip install pyansys-tools-report

   pip install git+https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git

   git clone https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git
   cd pyansys-tools-report
   pip install .

   unzip pyansys-tools-report-v0.9.dev0-wheelhouse-Linux-3.10.zip wheelhouse
   pip install pyansys-tools-report -f wheelhouse --no-index --upgrade --ignore-installed

   git clone https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git
   cd pyansys-tools-report
   pip install -e .[doc]

   make -C doc html

   cd doc/_build/html

   make -C doc clean

   git clone https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git
   cd pyansys-tools-report
   pip install -e .[test]

   pytest

   cd pyansys-tools-report
   pip install .

title: PyAnsys Tools Report > Offline installation
text:
If you lack an internet connection on your install machine, the recommended way
of installing PyAnsys Tools Report is downloading the wheelhouse archive from the
Releases Page for your
corresponding machine architecture.
Each wheelhouse archive contains all the python wheels necessary to install
PyAnsys Tools Report from scratch on Windows and Linux for Python >=3.10. You can install
this on an isolated system with a fresh python or on a virtual environment.
For example, on Linux with Python 3.10, unzip it and install it with the following:
If you’re on Windows with Python 3.10, unzip to a wheelhouse directory and
install using the same command as before.
Consider installing using a virtual environment.
More information on general PyAnsys development can be found in the
PyAnsys Developer’s Guide.
   unzip pyansys-tools-report-v0.9.dev0-wheelhouse-Linux-3.10.zip wheelhouse
   pip install pyansys-tools-report -f wheelhouse --no-index --upgrade --ignore-installed

title: PyAnsys Tools Report > Rendering the docs
text:
In case you were interested in rendering the docs locally, you need to clone the repository and
install the docs requirements first:
Once you have the requirements, render the docs by running the following:
This generates the HTML version of the docs, which you may find in the following directory:
You can also clean the build directory by running the following command:
   git clone https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git
   cd pyansys-tools-report
   pip install -e .[doc]

   make -C doc html

   cd doc/_build/html

   make -C doc clean

title: PyAnsys Tools Report > Running the tests
text:
In case you were interested in running the tests locally, you need to clone the repository and
install the test requirements first:
Once you have the requirements, run the tests by running the following:
The pyproject.toml file already contains some default configuration for running the tests. Please,
take a look at it if you may want to run it with your own configuration.
   git clone https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git
   cd pyansys-tools-report
   pip install -e .[test]

   pytest

title: PyAnsys Tools Report > Requirements
text:
This Python package does not contain specific requirements files. Instead, its requirements may
be found within the pyproject.toml file which defines the package. Thus, when the package is
installed it automatically detects the dependencies needed and installs them.

title: Contributing
text:
Guidance on contributing to a PyAnsys library appears in the
Contributing topic
in the PyAnsys Developer’s Guide. Ensure that you are thoroughly familiar with
it before attempting to contribute to the PyAnsys Tools Report repository.
The following contribution information is specific to the PyAnsys Tools Report repository.
Run this code to clone and install the latest version of the repository in development
mode:
In case of looking for the test requirements, run the following:
To build the documentation locally you need to follow these steps at the root
directory of the repository:
After the build completes the HTML documentation locates itself in the
_builds/html directory and you can load the index.html into a web
browser. To clean the documentation you can execute this command:
Use the PyAnsys Tools Report Issues page to
submit questions, report bugs, and request new features.
PyAnsys Tools Report is compliant with PyAnsys Development Code Style Guide.  Code style checks use
pre-commit. Install this tool and
activate it executing the following commands:
Then, you can make used of the available configuration file .pre-commit-config.yml,
which will be automatically detected by pre-commit:
git clone https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git
cd pyansys-tools-report
pip install .
pip install .[test]
pip install .[doc]
make -C doc html
make -C doc clean
python -m pip install pre-commit
pre-commit install
pre-commit run --all-files --show-diff-on-failure

title: Contributing > Cloning the PyAnsys Tools Report repository
section: Cloning the PyAnsys Tools Report repository
text:
Run this code to clone and install the latest version of the repository in development
mode:
In case of looking for the test requirements, run the following:
git clone https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-report.git
cd pyansys-tools-report
pip install .
pip install .[test]

title: Contributing > Building documentation
text:
To build the documentation locally you need to follow these steps at the root
directory of the repository:
After the build completes the HTML documentation locates itself in the
_builds/html directory and you can load the index.html into a web
browser. To clean the documentation you can execute this command:
pip install .[doc]
make -C doc html
make -C doc clean

title: Contributing > Posting issues
text:
Use the PyAnsys Tools Report Issues page to
submit questions, report bugs, and request new features.

title: Contributing > Code style
text:
PyAnsys Tools Report is compliant with PyAnsys Development Code Style Guide.  Code style checks use
pre-commit. Install this tool and
activate it executing the following commands:
Then, you can make used of the available configuration file .pre-commit-config.yml,
which will be automatically detected by pre-commit:
python -m pip install pre-commit
pre-commit install
pre-commit run --all-files --show-diff-on-failure

title: API reference
text:
This is the API reference documentation for the pyansys-tools-report package
PyAnsys Tools Report package.
Generate a scooby.Report instance.
List of packages or package names to add to output information.
Defaults to None.
Number of package-columns in html table; only has effect if
mode='HTML' or mode='html'. Defaults to 3.
The text width for non-HTML display modes. Defaults to 80.
Alphabetically sort the packages. Defaults to False.
Gather information about the GPU. Defaults to True but if
experiencing rendering issues, pass False to safely generate
a report.
List containing the Ansys environment variables to be reported.
(e.g. [“MYVAR_1”, “MYVAR_2” …]). Defaults to None.
Dictionary containing the Ansys libraries and versions to be reported.
(e.g. {“MyLib” : “v1.2”, …}). Defaults to None.
Return information regarding the Ansys environment and installation.
The project information (env variables and installation)
Return the version of the PyAnsys Report Tool.
The version of the tool being used.
